comments:
Summary
This paper presents a k-nearest-neighbors based portfolio (ArgoCSP-kNN) for finite linear CSPs, investigates the effect of training timeout on portfolio effectiveness, and proposes a practical "short training" use-case in which the portfolio is trained briefly on the very same fixed instance set the user wants to solve. The work includes extensive experiments on a large corpus (8436 instances) and many solvers/encodings, comparisons with state-of-the-art CSP portfolios (SUNNY, Proteus), analysis of different ML techniques (k-NN, linear regression, SVM), and a small transfer study to SAT instances.

Strengths
- Practical and well-motivated contribution: the short-training idea addresses a realistic scenario (solve one fixed corpus quickly with available solvers) and is well justified.
- Extensive empirical evaluation on a large, diverse instance set and a broad set of solvers/encodings; inclusion of comparisons to SUNNY and Proteus is appropriate and informative.
- The paper carefully studies the trade-off between training timeout and resulting portfolio performance, showing strong gains even with very small training timeouts.
- Demonstrates that k-NN is a competitive, efficient choice for the short-training scenario; discusses practical considerations (SVM too slow for short training).
- The authors acknowledge limitations (overlap between training and test in short-training scenario) and present comparisons to full cross-validation to quantify trade-offs.

Weaknesses / Concerns
- The short-training approach necessarily trains and runs all solvers on the corpus during preparation (albeit with small timeouts). For very large corpora or when users have very limited compute, even short timeouts across many solvers may be costly. Some quantitative guidance or heuristics for selecting a training timeout given a global time budget would strengthen practical usability (the authors mention this as future work).
- Although the paper compares with SUNNY and Proteus, statistical tests of significance are not reported. Given some differences are small, significance testing would clarify whether observed improvements are meaningful.
- The treatment of instances on which solvers returned incorrect answers is terse: 541 instances were excluded because at least one solver returned a wrong satisfiability answer. More detail on the nature of these errors, and how excluding them might bias the corpus, would be helpful.
- Feature analysis is limited: the paper uses a set of 70 features (and a reduced 28-feature set in some experiments) but provides little detail about which features are most predictive. A short ablation or feature-importance discussion would improve interpretability and guide future use.
- Some practical reproducibility details appear partially missing in the provided text (though a code/instance link is mentioned, third-party solver binaries are excluded for licensing reasons). Full instructions to reproduce the core results (scripts, seeds, exact solver configurations) would be beneficial.
- The provided manuscript text contained some duplicated/garbled lines (likely due to the PDF-to-text extraction), which slightly impairs readability—but the scientific content and experimental narrative remain clear.

Suggestions for improvement
- Add statistical significance testing (e.g., paired tests on solved counts or PAR10) when comparing to baselines.
- Provide more detail on how to pick a training timeout based on a user’s total available time (an automated heuristic or cost-benefit plot would be useful).
- Report more on feature importance/ablation to show which features drive k-NN performance and whether a small subset suffices in practice.
- Discuss the excluded 541 instances in more detail (types of solver errors) and whether their exclusion could bias results.
- Provide a more complete reproducibility package or a clear recipe listing solver versions/configuration and commands (even if third-party solvers must be obtained separately).

Overall assessment
This is a solid, practically motivated experimental paper. It does not propose a fundamentally new machine learning algorithm, but it proposes a useful methodology (short training on the target corpus) and demonstrates through thorough experiments that substantial gains are possible with very limited training time. The comparisons to SUNNY and Proteus and the SAT transfer experiments strengthen the paper. I recommend acceptance for publication; the presentation would be improved by addressing the points above (statistical tests, feature analysis, practical guidance for timeout selection, and clearer reproducibility instructions).
